lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market manufacturers of wrapping, bottling, canning, labeling, filling, bagging, case-packing, palletizing, inspection, and other packaging machinery that provide recurring or repeat outsourced service through installation, maintenance, spare parts, upgrades, remote support, monitoring, software, training, performance agreements, or lifecycle optimization.
lens.excluded_activities: Pure integrators and distributors without in-scope manufacturing, contract packaging companies, manufacturers limited to one-time machine sales without recurring external service obligations, captive internal units, shells, non-control financing vehicles, and firms outside the lower-middle-market band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Manufacturers sell standard and custom machines or complete lines to food, beverage, pharmaceutical, personal-care, chemical, consumer-product, and industrial producers. Eligible firms monetize the installed base through parts, field service, preventive and predictive maintenance, upgrades, training, digital monitoring, remote support, software, and multi-period performance or lifecycle agreements.

a.bridge: NIST describes AI tasks across US manufacturing, and Krones describes AI and digital tools in a large packaging-machinery group, rather than measuring the wage-weighted task mix of eligible firms. I map predictive maintenance, vision inspection, forecasting, scheduling, document processing, design assistance, remote diagnostics, and service analytics to engineering, assembly planning, commissioning, support, parts, and lifecycle work. Physical fabrication, assembly, installation, validation, and field service limit substitution, while engineering intensity may make broad manufacturing evidence understate exposed technical-office work.
a.rationale: AI can reduce engineering iteration, quoting, scheduling, inspection, technical-document work, spare-parts forecasting, commissioning support, and remote diagnostics. Building, integrating, installing, validating, and repairing physical packaging lines still requires accountable skilled labor. [S5] [S6]
a.change_evidence: Occupation-level payroll and task-time studies from representative packaging OEMs, separated across engineering, factory, commissioning, parts, administration, and field service, would materially change the range.
caveats: No task-exposure measurement specific to NAICS 333993 was found.
Custom line builders and standardized machine manufacturers have different engineering and service mixes.

rho.bridge: PMMI and Krones document packaging-sector AI, data collection, predictive maintenance, remote service, and implementation friction, while NIST and Census provide broader manufacturing and business adoption evidence. I translate these observations to five-year implementation for eligible firms, discounting for legacy installed equipment, customer cybersecurity, data rights, validation, integration cost, and skills. Large-OEM digital capability may overstate lower-middle-market readiness, but direct industry use cases reduce technology uncertainty.
rho.rationale: The sector has practical data from connected lines and clear applications in predictive maintenance, changeovers, inspection, scheduling, documentation, and remote support. Slow predictive-maintenance adoption, customer caution, legacy machines, cyber risk, validation requirements, and fragmented data constrain implementation. [S2] [S5] [S6] [S7]
rho.change_evidence: A PMMI survey of eligible OEMs covering connected installed base, remote-access permissions, software and service data, AI pilots, deployment budgets, cybersecurity, and measured production adoption would materially refine the estimate.
caveats: Public evidence mixes end-user and OEM adoption.
Large global suppliers have more digital resources than most screened firms.

e.rationale: Packaging machinery requires installation, commissioning, maintenance, parts, upgrades, format changes, training, and downtime support, creating recurring obligations around a long-lived installed base. PMMI explicitly tracks aftermarket parts, services, and predictive maintenance, while manufacturers promote lifecycle agreements and lifetime support; firms that cede all aftermarket work or only ship machines remain ineligible. [S1] [S3] [S4] [S5]
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census of lower-middle-market packaging OEMs, with revenue and contracts coded for parts, maintenance, remote service, software, upgrades, training, and performance agreements, would directly determine eligibility.
caveats: Global lifecycle-service leaders may overstate service attachment among smaller US OEMs.
Parts sales alone may not qualify without a substantive outsourced-service obligation.

s5.bridge: Duravant's completed acquisition of a privately held packaging-automation manufacturer and service provider demonstrates strategic control-transfer demand but does not measure the five-year transfer probability of eligible US lower-middle-market firms. I use the transaction as evidence of buyer appetite for engineered equipment plus aftermarket capabilities, then discount because the target was foreign, mature, and not denominator-matched. A strategic platform's cross-border acquisition may overstate liquidity for small domestic firms.
s5.rationale: A current completed acquisition shows that strategic buyers value packaging automation, custom engineering, and lifecycle service capabilities together. The eligible US transfer denominator, owner ages, and broader deal frequency remain unobserved. [S4]
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, Axial, or BizBuySell transactions mapped to NAICS 333993, combined with PMMI member owner-age and succession interviews, would materially change this probability.
caveats: The observed target was headquartered outside the US and may exceed the screened size band.
One strategic transaction does not establish a denominator-matched transfer rate.

q.rationale: Installed-base knowledge, proprietary controls, spare-parts availability, validation history, downtime risk, remote access, and performance responsibility create meaningful switching costs. Savings can still be shared through competitive capital bids, cost-plus customization, service renewal, uptime guarantees, customer procurement, third-party parts, and demands for measurable line-efficiency improvement. [S4] [S5]
q.change_evidence: Machine and service contracts, SLA penalties, renewal and churn data, parts margins, upgrade economics, bid histories, and customer interviews on alternative service providers would materially narrow the range.
caveats: Public sources emphasize leading lifecycle-service models rather than average lower-middle-market contract terms.
Retention differs materially between proprietary integrated lines and stand-alone standardized machines.

d5.bridge: PMMI reports current US packaging-machinery sales and a near-term nominal growth forecast and identifies aftermarket and automation trends, while Krones reports global company growth and robust investment. These indicators are not a constant-price five-year quantity forecast for eligible US services. I center the estimate on modest real expansion from automation, labor constraints, SKU complexity, installed-base service, and packaging change, while allowing cyclicality and capital deferral in the low case. Nominal machinery sales may overstate real quantity during inflation.
d5.rationale: Packaging machinery serves recurring production in food, beverage, pharmaceuticals, chemicals, and consumer goods. Automation, workforce constraints, changing formats, sustainability, high SKU counts, and demand for uptime support growth, while tariffs, capital costs, customer caution, and delayed predictive-maintenance adoption limit the outlook. [S2] [S3] [S5]
d5.change_evidence: PMMI's full US shipment forecast and aftermarket forecast, segmented by machine type and adjusted for prices, plus installed-base service attachment and customer capital budgets, would materially change this ratio.
caveats: The PMMI public forecast is near-term and nominal, not a five-year constant-price service forecast.
Large beverage-line suppliers and niche lower-middle-market OEMs can experience different demand cycles.

o.rationale: Year-five demand still requires an accountable manufacturer and service organization to engineer, build, install, validate, maintain, upgrade, and support physical packaging lines. AI and remote tools enhance that operator rather than replace it; modest leakage can arise from customer maintenance teams, independent integrators, third-party parts, software vendors, or standardized imports. [S1] [S4] [S5]
o.change_evidence: End-user interviews quantifying self-maintenance, independent service, third-party parts, software-only optimization, OEM service attachment, and direct imported equipment would materially change the estimate.
caveats: The accountable-operator share is not directly measured.
Proprietary high-speed lines and simple stand-alone machines face different self-service risks.

narrative.executive_view: Packaging-machinery manufacturing has a naturally service-rich installed base: commissioning, parts, maintenance, upgrades, training, remote support, and performance agreements can recur for years. AI fits engineering and lifecycle workflows well, but diligence must confirm that the manufacturer owns the customer service relationship and proprietary installed-base access.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can assist machine design, quoting, changeovers, visual inspection, scheduling, predictive maintenance, spare-parts forecasting, documentation, commissioning, and remote diagnostics. Physical fabrication, installation, validation, repair, and uptime accountability remain operator-intensive.
narrative.value_capture: Proprietary controls, validation history, installed-base knowledge, urgent downtime response, and performance responsibility support retention. Competitive equipment bids, uptime guarantees, service renewal, cost-plus projects, third-party parts, and customer procurement can transfer savings into price or stronger service commitments.
narrative.firm_availability: The dataset indicates a meaningful lower-middle-market population, and a current strategic acquisition demonstrates buyer appetite for packaging automation plus aftermarket service. Owner succession, US deal denominators, and each firm's actual service attachment remain unmeasured.
narrative.demand_durability: Packaging equipment supports essential, recurring production across diverse end markets. Automation, labor constraints, format changes, sustainability, SKU complexity, and a growing installed base support demand, while capital costs, tariffs, and customer hesitation create cyclical downside.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Key risks include project overruns, customer concentration by niche, food or pharmaceutical validation, cyber access, legacy controls, field-service labor, component shortages, tariffs, warranty obligations, and customers using independent integrators. Eligibility, service contract quality, transfer frequency, and AI-ready data are the decisive gaps.
narrative.principal_driver: Long-lived installed lines create recurring high-value parts, maintenance, upgrade, monitoring, and performance workflows with clear AI applications.
narrative.principal_weakness: Custom projects can be cyclical and contractually demanding, while customers or independent integrators may own much of the recurring service relationship.

sources.title: 2022 Economic Census Packaging Machinery Classification Form
sources.supports: Official classification of packaging machinery manufacturing, including wrapping, bottling, canning, and labeling machinery.

sources.title: 2025 Packaging State of the Industry: U.S. Expands, Canada Maintains Strong Base
sources.supports: Current US market size and growth, labor constraints, AI, predictive-maintenance adoption, sustainability, policy uncertainty, SKU complexity, and downtime needs.

sources.title: PMMI State of the Industry 2025
sources.supports: US and Canadian shipment research, aftermarket forecasts for parts and services, predictive maintenance, automation, and demand across packaging-machine categories.

sources.title: Duravant Completes Acquisition of Pattyn
sources.supports: Completed strategic acquisition of a private packaging-automation manufacturer and evidence of turnkey lines, lifetime aftermarket support, parts, consumables, and service programs.

sources.title: Krones 2025 Annual Report: Strategy and Management System
sources.supports: Lifecycle service agreements, digital support, remote maintenance, performance responsibility, stable service revenue, internal AI use, and packaging-machine lifecycle strategy.

sources.title: The Rise of Artificial Intelligence in U.S. Manufacturing
sources.supports: Manufacturing AI use cases in predictive maintenance, quality, forecasting, scheduling, document management, and design, plus implementation barriers.

sources.title: AI Use at U.S. Businesses
sources.supports: Current business AI adoption and expected-use evidence by firm size, used only as a cross-industry implementation proxy.
